content.body: i don't think it matters if it works with parseFloat or not; anyone using those functions with unbounded input is already pretty screwed

all else being equal, I think there's benefit in picking a format that never produces non-exceptional output from `parseFloat`/`JSON.parse`/etc. And harm in a format that produces non-exceptional output _most_ of the time but not in the general case, as noted [above](https://matrix.to/#/!NnKyFxSjabKpyjbPcL:matrix.org/$3Wz77dLsgCdcgmtdnQtpXsxVGDqmx9YSr6nu6D0Fvxw?via=matrix.org&via=igalia.com&via=mozilla.org)
content.formatted_body: all else being equal, I think there's benefit in picking a format that never produces non-exceptional output from parseFloat/JSON.parse/etc. And harm in a format that produces non-exceptional output most of the time but not in the general case, as noted above

content.body: all else being equal sure
ideally it either always produces NaN or always produces the intended number
but if it doesn't, that's ok
what would push us away from upholding that ideal?
how gnarly it forces the syntax to be :-) meaning, we should figure out the ideal aesthetic output first, and then, see what we'd need to tweak to meet the parseX goals
so to be clear: you think the subjective aesthetics of a string format take priority over ensuring consistent behavior that makes misuse obvious?
do you consider the examples at https://matrix.to/#/!NnKyFxSjabKpyjbPcL:matrix.org/$ISSj4yKoUkiMBT4U7wq1FX4XRSwUVpqvyaQBmhMRvw4?via=matrix.org&via=igalia.com&via=mozilla.org to be gnarly?
for parseInt and parseFloat, which are very difficult to NOT obviously misuse? yes.
i mean i think the brackets are weird

the whole point of an Amount is that you don't need a string representation, except for over the wire, so as long as there's a way to detect compliance and create an Amount from a string, then why would anybody try to parse for it?
(meaning i think Amount should have a static predicate for "is an amount string" and also a way, constructor or static factory, to coerce that string to an Amount)
and if those things exist, then there'd be no call for parseX, so they have no bearing on the result

I think we are operating under different definitions of "obvious"... I would say that most, and perhaps almost all, uses of parseInt and parseFloat are not **obviously** wrong precisely because they tend to work as authors intend _most_ of the time
content.formatted_body: I think we are operating under different definitions of "obvious"... I would say that most, and perhaps almost all, uses of parseInt and parseFloat are not obviously wrong precisely because they tend to work as authors intend most of the time

content.body: i mean starting the string with any nondigit character seems like it serves the goal of "always NaN", right?
yes, for `parseFloat`
well, non-digit non-`.`
parseInt also?
I think so, at least with unspecified radix
ok fair, i guess it'd have to be "not [a-zA-Z.]" to cover all radixes
* ok fair, i guess it'd have to be "not \[0-9a-zA-Z.\]" to cover all radixes

content.body: but yes, the general point is that a prefix chosen from a large menu of available punctuation characters accomplishes the goal
